narrative:
Luz Rivas represents California's 29th Congressional District, a working-class stretch of the San Fernando Valley that includes Pacoima, Sun Valley, and Sylmar. She is the first Latina to represent the district and the only Latina in Congress with a STEM background, holding a bachelor's in electrical engineering from MIT and a master's in education from Harvard [1][2]. Before entering politics, she worked as a design engineer at Motorola and founded DIY Girls, a nonprofit teaching engineering to girls in underserved communities [3]. She served six years in the California State Assembly before winning her House seat in 2024, and her freshman peers elected her to serve in House Democratic Leadership as Freshman Leadership Representative [4]. It is, on paper, a biography custom-built for the kind of populist authenticity the 80% Bill demands.

On the substance, Rivas checks several of the right boxes. She is a member of the Congressional Progressive Caucus, which has collectively endorsed banning congressional stock trading, expanding Medicare drug negotiation, passing paid family leave, and overturning Citizens United [5][6]. She sits on the Natural Resources Committee and the Science, Space, and Technology Committee, where she has focused on environmental justice and clean energy -- issues adjacent to the 80% Bill's farmland protections and Pentagon accountability planks [7]. In the California Assembly, she earned a 97% lifetime score from California Environmental Voters and secured over $70 million in state funding for infrastructure, education, and healthcare in her district [8][9]. She has introduced the Housing Without Fear Act of 2026 and a multigenerational housing tax credit bill, gestures toward the affordable housing crisis that dovetails with the 80% Bill's call to ban corporate purchase of single-family homes [10]. But on the core governance reforms -- the stock trading ban, the lifetime lobbying ban, term limits, the Pentagon audit, the DISCLOSE Act -- her name is conspicuously absent from cosponsor lists, even as dozens of her Democratic colleagues have signed on [11][12].

The money side of the ledger tells a familiar story. Rivas accepted more than $55,000 from pro-Israel PACs during the 2024 cycle, and in August 2024 she joined a delegation of fifteen House Democrats on an AIPAC-funded trip to Israel [13][14]. For a member of the Progressive Caucus who presumably supports getting corporate money out of politics, taking a junket underwritten by one of the most powerful lobbying organizations in Washington is, at minimum, an awkward look. Her overall fundraising operation for 2024 was modest by congressional standards -- roughly $575,000 -- but the willingness to accept PAC money while belonging to a caucus that rhetorically opposes it is precisely the kind of contradiction the 80% Bill is designed to surface [15].

Rivas is still early in her congressional career, and her Assembly record suggests genuine progressive instincts on climate, housing, and education. But the 80% Bill is not a test of instincts; it is a test of action on twenty specific reforms that roughly 80% of Americans support. On the issues where her committees give her jurisdiction -- clean energy, public lands, scientific oversight -- she is engaged. On the structural reforms that would actually change how Washington operates -- banning stock trades, killing dark money, auditing the Pentagon, imposing term limits -- she has yet to put her name on the line. The question for Rivas is whether the engineer in her will eventually insist on fixing the system itself, or whether the freshman in her will keep quietly deferring to the institution she just joined.

Representative Emily Randall arrived in Congress in January 2025 as the first queer Latina member of the House, a former state senator, and a healthcare advocate whose origin story—her sister Olivia's access to expanded Medicaid—reads like the opening chapter of a progressive policy textbook. She promptly joined the Congressional Progressive Caucus and declared her commitment to affordable healthcare, reproductive freedom, gun reform, and a green economy. On paper, she is exactly the kind of freshman who should be a natural champion of every plank in the 80% Bill.

So far, the early returns are promising but incomplete. Randall has voted against Republican spending packages, opposed the DOGE-backed rescission bills, and voted against the FY2026 DHS funding package, citing concerns about accountability and ICE overreach. Her healthcare advocacy background aligns naturally with Medicare drug negotiation and paid family leave. In the Washington State Senate, she led passage of legislation protecting abortion access, expanding Medicaid, and improving apprenticeship programs—a track record that suggests genuine progressive convictions rather than campaign-season cosplay.

But Congress has a way of filing down sharp edges, and Randall's first year reveals some early warning signs. She has missed 20 of 440 roll call votes (4.5%), a rate significantly worse than the median 2.0% for House members—not exactly the attendance record of someone burning to transform government. Her campaign raised $1.3 million in the 2024 cycle, with about 76.6% from individual donors according to FEC disclosures, but the remaining quarter invites scrutiny about which PACs and industries are investing in her future.

On the 80% Bill's specific priorities, Randall's freshman status means she has not yet had the opportunity—or perhaps the inclination—to co-sponsor legislation on congressional stock trading bans, Pentagon audits, term limits, the DISCLOSE Act, or banning corporate home buying. These are not obscure niche issues; they are the broadly popular reforms that 80% of Americans support. A progressive caucus membership card is not a substitute for a legislative record.

Randall represents a district that includes the Kitsap Peninsula, Olympia, and Joint Base Lewis-McChord—a mix of military families, union workers, and progressive communities that should be fertile ground for the 80% Bill agenda. The question is whether she will use her platform to champion these supermajority-popular reforms, or whether she will settle into the comfortable routine of voting "no" on Republican bills while sponsoring nothing that challenges her own party's donor class. Her sister Olivia's story got her here. What she does next will determine whether that story was a guiding principle or a campaign ad.

Josh Riley is a former corporate attorney at Boies Schiller Flexner — where he defended Uber and GoDaddy against patent claims — who arrived in Congress in January 2025 pledging to fight corruption and stand up for the little guy. The career arc from billing $800-an-hour for multinational corporations to railing against corporate greed is the kind of character development that only Washington can produce with a straight face.

To his credit, Riley has put real legislation behind the populist branding. He introduced the ETHICS Act alongside AOC, Raja Krishnamoorthi, and a bipartisan coalition to ban congressional stock trading for members, their spouses, and dependents. He co-signed the Protecting American Agriculture from Foreign Adversaries Act, which would block entities like CCP-linked Syngenta from gobbling up American farmland by adding the Secretary of Agriculture to CFIUS. When the USDA announced $16 billion in disaster relief, Riley fired off a letter demanding the money go to struggling family farms rather than Wall Street-backed agricultural giants. A man who once defended Uber now fights for dairy farmers — the Upstate New York conversion is complete.

His campaign finance record is genuinely clean. Riley pledged to refuse all corporate PAC money and the receipts confirm it. His 2024 campaign raised roughly $5.9 million, outraising his opponent by $3.5 million, largely through individual donors. His former law firms — Boies Schiller Flexner and Jenner & Block — contributed just under $176,000 through employee donations, which is not corporate PAC money but does suggest his old colleagues still have his number.

Riley sits on the House Agriculture Committee and the Science, Space, and Technology Committee — assignments that actually align with his district of rural Upstate New York towns and family farms. He introduced a bipartisan bill to update the Weatherization Assistance Program for low-income households and pushed legislation to expand financial aid to poultry producers affected by avian flu. He also introduced the Cracking Down on Price Gouging Act, a national ban on price gouging during crises.

His stated priorities include fighting corruption, defending abortion rights, securing the border, and lowering costs. He is a member of the New Democrat Coalition, the caucus for Democrats who want to signal they are "pragmatic" without specifying what they are being pragmatic about. His voting attendance is solid — missing only 12 of 449 roll calls (2.7%) through early 2026.

The tension in Riley's record is the gap between the corporate lawyer past and the populist reformer present. He talks about getting dark money out of politics and overturning Citizens United, which is encouraging from someone who spent a decade in elite law firms that profit handsomely from the current system. Whether he is a genuine convert or simply a shrewd politician who read the polling, the legislation he is filing mostly points in the right direction.

Bernie Sanders is the closest thing the U.S. Senate has to a living proof-of-concept for the 80% Bill -- which makes it all the more maddening that after four decades in politics, most of these policies remain theoretical. The Independent from Vermont has been saying the same things since the Reagan administration, and the fact that America is only now catching up is either a testament to his prescience or a damning indictment of the system he's been inside of for 35 years. Possibly both.

On taxing the ultra-wealthy, Sanders is the undisputed heavyweight champion. His Make Billionaires Pay Their Fair Share Act, introduced with Rep. Ro Khanna in March 2026, would slap a 5% annual wealth tax on America's 938 billionaires, projecting $4.4 trillion in revenue. His Corporate Tax Dodging Prevention Act would close offshore tax havens in the Cayman Islands and Luxembourg, raising another trillion over a decade. The man does not lack for ambition -- just the 60 votes needed to pass anything.

On auditing the Pentagon, Sanders teamed up with Republican Chuck Grassley to introduce the Audit the Pentagon Act after the DOD failed its seventh consecutive audit. Defense contractors overcharging taxpayers by 40-50% -- and sometimes 4,451% -- is the kind of bipartisan outrage that should unite everyone, yet only 13 senators voted against the bloated defense budget. Sanders was one of them.

Medicare drug negotiation? Sanders chaired the HELP Committee from 2023-2025 and has been introducing drug pricing bills since before some of his Senate colleagues were born. He backed the Inflation Reduction Act's negotiation provisions and continues pushing to expand them. On ending forever wars, he's introduced multiple resolutions to block arms sales to Israel, garnering growing support from 15 to 27 Senate Democrats.

The man refuses corporate PAC money, runs on small-dollar donations, and maintains a 68% approval rating in Vermont. His campaign finance purity is genuine -- though Common Cause did file a complaint against his allied group Our Revolution for skirting soft money rules, proving that even Sanders's orbit isn't entirely immune to the gravitational pull of dark money.

Sanders is the rare politician who actually means what he says on nearly every plank of the 80% Bill. The tragedy is that meaning it and accomplishing it remain frustratingly different things in the United States Senate.

Chuck Schumer has been in Congress since 1981, which means he has been accepting Wall Street donations for longer than most of his constituents have been alive. The Senate Minority Leader — and previously Majority Leader — has collected over $13 million from the securities and investment industry across his career, with Goldman Sachs ($543,000), Citigroup ($484,000), and JPMorgan Chase ($365,000) leading the charge. By 2009, he was personally vacuuming up 15 percent of all Wall Street donations made to members of Congress. His OpenSecrets page reads less like a campaign finance report and more like a Bloomberg terminal.

To his credit, Schumer shepherded the Inflation Reduction Act through the Senate, which for the first time allowed Medicare to negotiate drug prices directly with pharmaceutical companies — a genuine win covering ten high-cost drugs and saving seniors an estimated $1.5 billion per year. He pushed the Social Security Fairness Act to eliminate benefit limits on public service workers. These are real accomplishments that align with several 80% Bill policies.

But then there is the other Schumer. He gave passionate floor speeches demanding passage of the DISCLOSE Act to "fight the cancer of dark money in our elections." Stirring words — from a man whose affiliated Senate Majority PAC accepted $81 million from Majority Forward, a dark money nonprofit, between 2021 and 2023. That is not irony; that is performance art. He denounces the corrupting influence of anonymous money while his political operation runs on it like a diesel engine.

On stock trading bans, Schumer has stayed conveniently above the fray, neither championing nor blocking legislation. On term limits, the man entering his fifth decade of federal service has been conspicuously silent. On Pentagon audits, the Department of Defense has failed its audit every single year since Congress mandated them in 2018, and Schumer has not made it a priority. His family ties to the financial sector — including a son-in-law at Blackstone — add another layer to the Wall Street relationship that defines his career.

Schumer is the embodiment of a particular Democratic archetype: progressive enough on the issues that cost him nothing, conveniently moderate on the ones that might upset his donor base. He will negotiate your drug prices down and then cash a check from BlackRock on the way home. New York deserves a senator who fights for all twenty policies, not just the ones that come with a fundraising upside.

Maria Elvira Salazar is a Republican representing Florida's 27th Congressional District, a heavily Latino swath of Miami-Dade and Monroe counties that includes Key West. Before entering politics, Salazar spent three decades as a broadcast journalist for Telemundo, CNN en Espanol, and Univision, winning five Emmy Awards and famously securing the first interview by a Hispanic journalist with Fidel Castro in 1995 [1]. She holds a Master of Public Administration from Harvard, flipped the seat from Democrat Donna Shalala in 2020, and now serves on the Foreign Affairs and Small Business committees [2]. She brands herself as a bridge-builder — a rare Republican willing to work across the aisle — though the bridge has some conspicuous toll booths.

Salazar's signature legislative achievement is the bipartisan Dignity Act, an immigration reform bill she co-leads with Democrat Veronica Escobar that offers a pathway for undocumented immigrants while mandating E-Verify and border security [3]. It is genuinely ambitious and genuinely bipartisan, which makes her record on the 80% Bill's twenty planks all the more puzzling. She has publicly stated that "the government can and should renegotiate drug prices to lower the high cost of medications" [4], yet she voted against the Inflation Reduction Act that actually implemented Medicare drug price negotiation [5]. She has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, end dark money, pass the DISCLOSE Act, ban corporate PACs, audit the Pentagon, impose a lifetime lobbying ban, or close the carried interest loophole. On paid family leave, right to repair, banning junk fees, and preventing corporate purchases of single-family homes, her legislative footprint is essentially nonexistent.

Salazar's most revealing quality may be her relationship with the truth about her own votes. In a now-infamous January 2024 interview on CBS News Miami, she was confronted with the fact that she had held photo-ops taking credit for $650,000 in funding from the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2023 — a bill she voted against [6]. Pressed further about taking credit for the CHIPS and Science Act and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, both of which she also opposed, Salazar replied, "I cannot really remember right now" and said she would need to ask her staff [7]. It was a masterclass in the political art of claiming credit for the meal while refusing to pay the check.

Follow the money and the pattern holds. Salazar's donors include Verizon ($7,000), UPS ($5,000), Boeing, Charles Schwab, the National Multifamily Housing Council, the American Bankers Association, and Comcast [8]. Her campaign also ran afoul of the Federal Election Commission, which flagged $147,400 in illegally excessive contributions during her 2022 cycle, including money from a Colombian lawyer connected to controversial international figures [9]. A subsequent FEC review found her former campaign treasurer had violated federal law by failing to properly deposit and report campaign receipts [10]. For someone who has not lifted a finger on campaign finance reform, the irony is thick.

Maria Elvira Salazar is in many ways the most interesting kind of congressional disappointment: a genuinely talented communicator with occasional bipartisan instincts who nonetheless declines to support the popular reforms that would inconvenience her donor class. She will champion immigration reform that costs corporate America nothing and skip the votes that would. The 80% Bill asks whether representatives serve the people or the money, and Salazar's answer — delivered with a smile, an Emmy-winning camera presence, and a conveniently faulty memory — is both.

Rep. Bobby Scott has been representing Virginia's 3rd District since 1993 — over three decades of seniority that makes him the dean of the Virginia delegation and a walking argument for term limits, even if he would never admit it. The first Filipino American voting member of Congress, Scott has built a career as the ranking Democrat on the Education and Workforce Committee, championing labor rights, minimum wage increases, and paid family leave. On paper, he is one of the good ones. Naturally, the fine print tells a more complicated story.

Scott has been a genuine champion of Medicare drug price negotiation, co-introducing H.R. 3, the Elijah E. Cummings Lower Drug Costs Now Act, alongside Frank Pallone and Richard Neal. The bill would empower the federal government to negotiate prescription drug prices and cap seniors' out-of-pocket costs at $2,000 per year. He has pushed for paid family leave, affordable childcare, and worker protections with the fervor of a man who has been in Congress long enough to remember when these were bipartisan ideas. His record on education funding, job training, and eliminating workplace discrimination is consistently progressive.

But then there is the stock trading. In 2021, the Campaign Legal Center filed an ethics complaint against Scott for failing to disclose four stock transactions in 2020 worth between $4,004 and $60,000 — purchases in Air Products and Chemicals, Carlisle Companies, Linde PLC, and RPM International. The STOCK Act requires these disclosures precisely so voters can see whether their representatives are trading on insider knowledge. Scott simply did not bother filing. The Campaign Legal Center noted that "the lack of accountability we've seen in regard to STOCK Act compliance is basically giving elected officials the green light to buy and sell stocks based on information gained from committee meetings without any transparency."

For a man who champions transparency and worker protections, the irony is thick enough to cut with a legislative markup pen. Scott supports many of the right policies — drug price negotiation, paid leave, fair elections — but his three decades in office and his own STOCK Act violations illustrate exactly why the 80% Bill includes both a congressional stock trading ban and term limits. You can fight for working families and still benefit from a system that lets you trade stocks in the shadows. Scott proves that even the "good" representatives are products of a broken system, one where 30 years of incumbency creates comfort, and comfort breeds the kind of casual rule-breaking that erodes public trust.

The 80% Bill does not ask whether Bobby Scott's heart is in the right place. It asks whether any human being should hold the same congressional seat for three decades while quietly failing to disclose their stock trades.

Pete Sessions has been in Congress for the better part of three decades, which is less a career and more a geological event. First elected in 1996, he lost his Dallas-area seat in 2018, then promptly moved to Waco and won a completely different district in 2020 — a maneuver that would be called carpetbagging if anyone in Texas politics still pretended to care about such things. He now serves on the Financial Services Committee and chairs the Oversight Subcommittee on Government Operations, positions that would be ironic even if he hadn't repeatedly violated the very transparency laws Congress wrote for itself.

In 2021, Business Insider reported that Sessions violated the STOCK Act by failing to disclose a purchase of Amazon stock on time. In 2022, he was caught again — this time late in reporting seven additional stock trades worth between $7,000 and $105,000, including positions in Microsoft, Kroger, Taiwan Semiconductor, and BlackRock funds. The most tardy disclosure was four months overdue. When Congress debated banning stock trading by its own members, Sessions opposed the idea. A man who cannot follow stock disclosure rules on time does not want stricter stock disclosure rules. Shocking.

Then there is the matter of "Congressman-1." In 2019, when Giuliani associates Lev Parnas and Igor Fruman were indicted on campaign finance charges, federal prosecutors described an unnamed congressman who accepted donations from the pair and then wrote a letter to Secretary of State Pompeo urging the removal of U.S. Ambassador to Ukraine Marie Yovanovitch. That congressman was Pete Sessions. He accepted the maximum $2,700 contributions, and the indictment alleged the men "committed to raise $20,000 or more" for him. Sessions wrote the letter. The ambassador was removed. Sessions says he had no idea about any scheme, which is the congressional equivalent of claiming the check is in the mail.

Meanwhile, the Texas Observer documented how Sessions' leadership PAC, PETE PAC, spent nearly a quarter-million dollars on luxury ski resort retreats at the St. Regis in Deer Valley, where corporate PAC checks from Koch Industries, JP Morgan Chase, Lockheed Martin, and tobacco companies like Altria conveniently arrived shortly after the slopes were enjoyed. He also voted to overturn the 2020 election results in Arizona and Pennsylvania just hours after the January 6th attack on the Capitol.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Sessions has no record supporting a stock trading ban (he actively opposes one while violating existing rules), no support for dark money disclosure, no support for banning corporate PACs, and no support for term limits — despite having been in office long enough to qualify for his own historical marker. He voted for the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that added $2.8 trillion to the deficit while sitting on the committee that oversees the nation's finances. Pete Sessions is what happens when Washington's revolving door gets stuck in the open position — and nobody bothers to fix it because the people walking through keep getting reelected.

Brad Sherman has represented the San Fernando Valley in Congress since 1997, making him one of the longest-serving members of the California delegation -- now in his fifteenth term and approaching three decades in the House [1]. A certified public accountant who worked at a Big Four firm before going to Harvard Law, Sherman is the only Democratic CPA in Congress and has spent 26 years on the House Financial Services Committee, where he currently serves as Ranking Member of the Capital Markets Subcommittee [2][3]. He is, in other words, the Democrat who is supposed to understand Wall Street better than anyone. Whether that understanding has translated into holding Wall Street accountable is the question the 80% Bill forces you to ask.

Sherman's record on the twenty bills is a study in partial credit. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the landmark Medicare drug price negotiation provision, and his office has touted his work protecting Social Security and Medicare from cuts [4][5]. He has advocated for banning congressional stock trading, declaring that "public service is a choice" that "requires a certain level of sacrifice" and emphasizing that he cares about "how they vote" rather than what people say [6]. He co-introduced the 8-K Trading Gap Act to close insider trading loopholes for corporate executives [7]. He proposed the Open Government Act in 2006, which would have banned gifts from lobbyists to members of Congress and tightened travel rules [8]. He co-sponsored H.R. 7802, which would amend the Federal Election Campaign Act to require additional disclosure from corporations, Super PACs, and dark money groups -- a step toward the DISCLOSE Act's goals [9]. And he has been one of Congress's most vocal critics of cryptocurrency, calling it "a garden of snakes" and "a crock," pushing the SEC to take enforcement action against major exchanges [10][11]. On consumer protection, he teamed with Elizabeth Warren and Katie Porter to challenge TurboTax for deceptive practices that forced taxpayers to pay for services that should be free [12].

But here is where the CPA's ledger gets interesting. Sherman has sat on the Financial Services Committee for over a quarter century -- the committee that directly oversees the banking, securities, insurance, and real estate industries. Those same industries happen to be his most generous donors. Over his career, Sherman has raised over $17 million, with top contributing sectors including securities and investment, real estate, insurance, and credit unions [13][14]. The Credit Union National Association is among his largest organizational contributors [14]. This is the congressional equivalent of a health inspector whose restaurant tabs are picked up by the establishments he inspects. Sherman has never championed closing the carried interest loophole, which allows private equity managers to pay capital gains rates on what is essentially labor income -- a reform that enjoys overwhelming public support but would directly affect his committee's most important constituents [15]. He introduced bills requiring Chinese companies to meet transparency standards before selling stock to American investors, but has been notably quieter about transparency requirements for the domestic financial firms that fund his campaigns [3].

On several 80% Bill items, Sherman's record is simply silent. He has not been a prominent voice on congressional term limits -- understandable, perhaps, for a man serving his fifteenth term. He has not taken a leading role on police body cameras, protecting U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, or the right to repair. He has not pushed for a lifetime lobbying ban. On the Pentagon audit, the nation's only Democratic CPA in Congress -- the man whose entire professional identity is built on making the numbers add up -- has been conspicuously absent from the fight to make the Department of Defense account for its $800 billion annual budget [16]. If there is anyone in Congress who should be leading the charge to audit the Pentagon, it is the guy who literally used to audit things for a living. The silence is deafening.

The bottom line on Sherman is that he is a competent, knowledgeable legislator who genuinely understands financial regulation and occasionally uses that knowledge in the public interest. But three decades on the committee that oversees Wall Street, funded by the industries he regulates, with a voting record that conveniently avoids the reforms that would most disrupt his donor base -- that is not oversight, it is a subscription service. Sherman knows where the bodies are buried in the tax code and the securities laws. He just has not dug many of them up.

Adam Smith has represented Washington's 9th District since 1997, which in congressional terms makes him roughly immortal. He's the ranking Democrat on the House Armed Services Committee -- formerly the chairman -- a position that grants him enormous power over the Pentagon's budget and, coincidentally, enormous access to defense contractor cash. Over his twenty-six-year career, Smith has collected more than $1.5 million from the military industry. His top career donors read like a weapons expo brochure: Boeing ($163,450), Northrop Grumman ($150,250), and Raytheon Technologies ($117,000). He even created a leadership PAC called ADAM PAC -- which stands for American Defense & Military -- funded almost entirely by defense contractors and lobbyists. Subtlety is not his strong suit.

Sixty percent of Smith's campaign donations come from PACs, a figure so high that the Seattle Times ran a story about it. When asked whether this avalanche of corporate money influences his votes, Smith offers the classic congressional response: it doesn't. He has "never been shy about taking positions or voting against the interests of people who donate to me," he says, which is technically true in the same way that a fox has never been shy about guarding a henhouse.

The evidence tells a different story. House members who voted to authorize $886 billion in military spending took four times more money from military contractors than those who voted against it. Smith not only voted yes but called fellow Democrats "extremists" for wanting to trim the Defense Department's budget. When progressives pushed to cut Pentagon spending by 10%, Smith was their loudest Democratic opponent -- all while ranking fourth in the entire House for arms industry contributions.

Then there's AIPAC, which dropped roughly $200,000 on Smith's 2024 campaign. As the top Democrat on military matters, Smith has significant say in arms sales and foreign military aid, making him a prime investment for the Israel lobby. He does not cosponsor ceasefire resolutions or resolutions to restrict precision-guided bomb transfers.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Smith is a case study in selective populism. He'll talk about fair elections and paid family leave, but the structural reforms -- ending forever wars, auditing the Pentagon, banning corporate PACs, imposing a lobbying ban -- would dismantle the very infrastructure that has kept him in power for nearly three decades. The Pentagon has failed its audit seven years running, and the man in charge of Democratic oversight has Boeing on speed dial. Adam Smith isn't broken; he's working exactly as designed -- just not for you.
